visualGoal: Make a Star Citizen pilot confident that a head tracker is the smarter, lower-friction look-around buy in 2026, while being fully honest that VR is real now but experimental and demanding. Real product proof for both camps (trackers + headsets); the two decision-support infographics below are the biggest visual wins Codex can add, since this is a low-product-density explainer that leans on tables and decision logic rather than product cards.

editorialCompositePrompts.prompt: Realistic dim home sim cockpit at night lit by monitor glow, split visual metaphor: on the left a pilot at a flat monitor with a small head-tracker clip catching IR light and a keyboard/HOTAS visible on the desk; on the right the same pilot in a VR headset with the desk fading to a starfield. Leave clean product-overlay space for a real TrackIR 5 photo on the desk side and a real Quest 3 photo on the headset side. No fake logos, no fake UI text, 16:9 editorial cover.
editorialCompositePrompts.negativePrompt: fake brand logos, warped hardware, impossible ergonomics, tiny unreadable text, motion-blur nausea imagery, procedural gradient art

infographicPrompts.title: Head tracking or VR? Decide in four questions
content: Q1: Playing on a monitor or committing to a headset? Monitor -> tracker branch. Headset -> VR branch.
Tracker branch: Mind wearing a small clip? Yes clip = TrackIR 5 (best accuracy, ~$150). No clip = Tobii Eye Tracker 5 (~$339 US). Not sure yet = SmoothTrack + OpenTrack (a few $).
VR branch: Strong GPU + OK with experimental + no motion controllers yet? Start cheap = Quest 3 (~$500, wireless PCVR). Want clarity = Pimax Crystal Light (~$899).
Reminder node: trackers and VR are mutually exclusive — pick one per session.
infographicPrompts.style: IgnitionSim command-center flowchart, high-contrast space palette (deep navy + magenta/cyan accents), large readable node text, real product thumbnails as leaf-node markers for Codex overlay (TrackIR, Tobii, Quest 3, Pimax)

infographicPrompts.title: The four look-around solutions, by cost and performance tax
content: SmoothTrack + OpenTrack — a few dollars — zero performance tax — fiddly setup
TrackIR 5 — ~$150 (Pro bundle ~$175-180) — zero performance tax — wear a clip
Tobii Eye Tracker 5 — ~$339 US — negligible tax — clip-free, shorter range
Meta Quest 3 — ~$500 — heavy tax (DLSS) — max immersion, wireless PCVR
Pimax Crystal Light — ~$899 — heavy tax (DLSS) — max clarity, tethered
infographicPrompts.style: Horizontal cost ladder left-to-right, a color-coded performance-tax bar under each rung (green=none -> red=heavy), product-photo placeholders on each rung for Codex overlays, 'check current price' footnote flag on the two tracker rungs and the two headset rungs

curatorScenePrompts.prompt: Realistic adult space-sim curator Val Chen in a tidy dark home cockpit bay lit by monitor glow, one hand on a HOSAS grip, thumb hovering over a stick button, a small head-tracker clip on the headset, keyboard visible on the desk, calm and practical energy, product-overlay space beside them, practical not fantasy, 4:5 portrait.

screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.need: Star Citizen Alpha 4.6 in-game VR settings toggle screenshot (the official menu option added in 4.6). Would concretely prove the '4.6 added an official VR toggle' claim. Must be a real screenshot the studio/community published — do NOT fabricate a UI mockup. Optional / nice-to-have.
Legitimate Star Citizen VR in-cockpit or EVA capture illustrating the sense of scale the VR case describes. Only use real, properly-sourced/licensed game media; otherwise skip. Optional.

openQuestions: Confirm exact current US price for TrackIR 5 (base) and TrackClip Pro bundle — body uses ~$150 / ~$175-180 with a check-current flag because trackir.com + Amazon are JS-rendered.
Confirm current US price for the Tobii Eye Tracker 5 after the 2026 US increase — body uses ~$339 from a Jan 2026 review; Tobii's own price-increase page 403'd.
Confirm current Quest 3 (~$500) and Pimax Crystal Light (~$899) street prices before certification.
Do we want the SC 4.6 VR-toggle screenshot embedded, or keep the article product-photo-only? (It would raise visualCount but requires real, sourced game media.)
